attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with several hours of bleeding per rectum. He reports suddenly passing a large volume of dark-red blood (estimated as several large cups) with some clots. He has not had any recent gastrointestinal symptoms. His medical history includes insertion of a coronary artery stent five years ago, for which he takes aspirin. Eight years ago, he had a duodenal ulcer related to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug use, which was treated with esomeprazole. He does not take any other regular medications.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 125/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Cardiovascular examination is unremarkable, apart from a soft ejection systolic murmur. Abdominal examination reveals no tenderness or masses. Digital rectal examination shows the presence of red-brown blood.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of the rectal bleeding?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding, characterised by the passage of large amounts of red-brown blood per rectum. The most likely cause in this scenario is diverticular disease. Diverticular disease is the most common cause of lower gastrointestinal bleeds presenting to hospital, accounting for approximately 30-50% of cases. It's important to note that bleeding from diverticular disease is often not related to diverticulitis.

While most episodes of diverticulitis occur in the left colon, the right colon is the most common site of diverticular bleeding. The pathological process involves the herniation and formation of a diverticulum through an area of colon wall weakness, produced by penetration of vasa recta. Over time, this vessel may be exposed to recurrent trauma, and with thinning, may rupture and bleed into the lumen.

Peptic ulcer disease is unlikely to cause per rectum bleeding as described here. Upper GI bleeding typically presents as melaena (black, tarry stools) due to the partial digestion of blood as it passes through the intestines. If recognizable blood is passed per rectum from an upper gastrointestinal tract bleed, it would imply a massive, rapid bleed that would almost certainly be associated with hemodynamic compromise, which is not evident in this patient.

Carcinoma of the colon, although possible, is a less common cause of visible bleeding than diverticular disease. Bleeding from colorectal cancer occurs due to erosion of the tumor into underlying vessels or as a result of ulceration and fragility of the cancer tissue. However, such bleeding is usually insidious and slow, not typically presenting as acute, large-volume bleeding.

Angiodysplasia of the colon is an uncommon cause of lower GI bleeding. This condition is characterized by dilated submucosal vessels that tend to bleed into the GI tract as they lack smooth muscle in the wall. Bleeding from angiodysplasia tends to be episodic and self-limiting, and not associated with discomfort. This patient has a murmur suggesting possible aortic stenosis, which can be associated with angiodysplasia in Heyde's syndrome, however this condition is rare and primarily affects elderly people with severe aortic stenosis; gastrointestinal bleeding in such cases is usually less acute. Diverticular bleeding is a far more common and likely cause of the large-volume bleeding described in this case.

Haemorrhoids often present with the passage of bright red blood on the stools or on toilet paper and is a common cause of rectal bleeding, but not typically with large amounts of blood as reported by this patient. They may be associated with pain, itching around the anus, and/or a palpable mass. The absence of these symptoms and the volume of bleeding make haemorrhoids an unlikely cause in this case.

In conclusion, while all of these conditions can cause rectal bleeding, diverticular disease remains the most likely cause given its high prevalence, the acute onset of significant bleeding, and the absence of other specific symptoms or signs pointing to alternative diagnoses.
learningPoints: Diverticular disease is the most common cause of lower gastrointestinal bleeding in patients presenting to hospital.
Right-sided diverticular disease is more likely to cause significant bleeding than left-sided disease.
Angiodysplasia is an uncommon cause of lower gastrointestinal bleeding, often associated with aortic stenosis (Heyde's syndrome).
references: Strate LL. Lower GI bleeding: epidemiology and diagnosis. Gastroenterol Clin North Am. 2005 Dec;34(4):643-64. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16303575/
Meyers MA, Alonso DR, Gray GF, Baer JW. Pathogenesis of bleeding colonic diverticulosis. Gastroenterology. 1976 Oct;71(4):577-83. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1085269/
